blocks.text: Prepared by Jeffrey Perez, Design Technician, for {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}. This report covers the period since the last report and states the position of the study at its midpoint.

The study is on schedule. The survey is complete, the base map is built, the counts are taken and analyzed, and the working alignment and typical section are drawn. The drainage analysis is under way against that alignment. Half the work is behind us and the half in front of us is the half that produces the report, which is the ordinary shape of a study of this kind and not a sign that the back end is thin. One item is not on schedule and it is not ours: the utility owner has not answered our requests for records of the overhead line at the north end of the corridor. That is discussed under risks below.

items: Field survey completed through the study limits and tied to county control. The base map is built from the survey. Where the survey and the county as-built drawings disagree, and they do at two locations, the survey governs and the disagreement is noted in the file rather than reconciled away.
Turning-movement and volume counts taken at the study intersections on ordinary weekdays with school in session. Capacity analysis completed for existing and projected conditions.
Working horizontal and vertical alignment drawn, and the typical section developed against it. Grading limits are established through the corridor except at the north end.
Culvert inventory and ditch cross sections extracted from the survey and furnished to Linda George for the drainage analysis.
Preliminary quantity takeoffs started against the working alignment. Takeoffs are checked once forward and once backward before any quantity enters the cost opinion.
Progress meeting held at your office to review the alignment, the drainage approach, and the utility conflicts.
Comparison exhibit showing the shifted alignment at the north end against the recommended alignment, with the taking on each parcel shown, so the trade is visible rather than argued.
Cross drain sizing at the low point and the replacement structure detail. Linda George's work.
Utility conflict summary, once the overhead line records are in hand.
Completion of the quantity takeoffs and the opinion of probable construction cost at the preliminary engineering level.

Draft report, written by Linda George, with the exhibits and quantity tables supplied by this office in the form she requires. The draft goes to Evans-Lee for factual review before it is sealed and issued.

blocks.text: The utility records are the risk that can move the schedule. Two written requests have gone out and neither has been answered. The requests and their dates are logged in the project file. A third goes out this week. If the records do not arrive, the study can still be completed, but the utility conflict summary would have to be qualified as based on field observation rather than on record information, and a qualified summary is worth less to Evans-Lee at permitting than an unqualified one. We would rather say this now than explain it in the report.

The second risk is the detention question. The widening adds impervious area, and whether the county requires detention for a roadway improvement of this scale depends on how the public works department reads its ordinance. It has been read both ways in this county. We are carrying detention in the cost opinion as a line item with the assumption noted, and Linda George will confirm the reading with the county before the draft report. If the requirement is confirmed, the number is already in the budget the client is looking at. If it is not, the line comes out. Either way it will not be a surprise at permitting.

The third is the north end right-of-way. The recommendation will call for the strip rather than a retaining structure, on the ground that a wall costs more to build than the strip costs to acquire and costs again every year somebody has to maintain it. Acquisition is not within our scope and it is not within our control, and the schedule for the study does not depend on it. The schedule for the improvement will. The comparison exhibit is being drawn so that the trade between the strip and the shifted alignment can be seen rather than argued, and so that whoever makes that decision makes it with the takings in front of him.

The lump sum fee of {{fact:f:E-2005-001.fee}} is unchanged, and we do not expect it to change. Approximately half of it is earned as of this report, which tracks the work completed. No services outside the scope have been performed and none have been authorized. The phasing recommendation raised at the progress meeting remains outside the scope; if it is authorized in writing, the fee for it is discussed before any of it is drawn.
